name: notable blue pod
shortDescription: A notable blue pod has a smooth shell and a color rare enough to make even experienced greengrocers pause. It may hold seeds, dye, or alchemical pulp, but its first trick is always convincing buyers it must be special.
flavorText: “Blue pods sell because the plant looks as though it knew a secret.”

name: notable blue and yellow bush
shortDescription: A notable blue and yellow bush is showy, cheerful, and difficult to ignore in any herbal stall. Its paired colors make it popular for festival cuttings, bright dyes, and tonics that benefit from looking happier than they taste.
flavorText: “Blue and yellow together make even a shrub look like a herald.”

name: notable cornish bulb
shortDescription: A notable cornish bulb is a firm, knuckled root with a pale heart and a reputation for surviving poor soil. Cooks treat it as sturdy food, healers as a grounding ingredient, and farmers as proof that ugly things can still be worth storing.
flavorText: “A good bulb is a promise buried deep enough to keep.”

name: bush
shortDescription: A plain bush is ordinary plant stock: leaves, stems, small twigs, and whatever useful secrets grow between them. Most buyers overlook it until they need fodder, kindling, hedging, dye leaves, or a place to hide something small.
flavorText: “A bush is never just a bush to someone who forgot rope, shade, or cover.”

name: bushy plant
shortDescription: A bushy plant offers more leaf than elegance, dense enough for clipping, drying, wrapping, or feeding to animals with flexible standards. It is not rare, but steady goods keep markets alive between the glamorous sales.
flavorText: “Bushy plants do the quiet work rare flowers take credit for.”

name: cattail
shortDescription: Cattail grows in wet places, tall and brown-tipped, useful from root to fluff if the gatherer knows the marsh. It feeds, pads, stuffs, and burns, making it one of those humble plants poor villages respect more than city ledgers do.
flavorText: “Cattail is the marsh saying yes in several languages.”

name: scarce delicate pink
shortDescription: Scarce delicate pink is a tender botanical with pale color and a habit of bruising under rough fingers. Perfumers and charm-makers pay well for it, not because it lasts, but because fragile things often smell like money.
flavorText: “Delicate pink petals teach merchants to breathe carefully.”

name: scarce red fat boy
shortDescription: Scarce red fat boy is a plump, red-stemmed plant with a ridiculous market name and surprisingly serious uses. Its swollen parts hold moisture and bitter sap, making it valuable to healers who pretend not to smile when ordering it.
flavorText: “A silly name does not make the root any cheaper.”

name: fruit branch
shortDescription: A fruit branch is a clipped piece of orchard promise, heavy with leaves, small fruit, or the scent of harvest still clinging to the bark. Graftsmen, cooks, and festival sellers all see different value in the same cut limb.
flavorText: “A fruit branch is a tree’s argument that tomorrow should be sweet.”

name: notable nightly
shortDescription: Notable nightly is a dark-leaved plant that seems livelier after sunset, when its scent strengthens and pale veins show in low light. Night gardeners, dream brewers, and suspicious poets all claim it has uses best not discussed before breakfast.
flavorText: “Some plants wait for the sun to leave before telling the truth.”

name: fabled plant of death
shortDescription: The fabled plant of death is rare, feared, and wrapped in more warnings than most royal decrees. Whether poison, omen, or powerful medicine depends on the handler, but no honest trader sells it without making the buyer repeat the name aloud.
flavorText: “A death plant may heal in the right dose, but it never stops introducing itself.”

name: large leaf
shortDescription: A large leaf is broad, flexible plant stock, useful for wrapping food, lining baskets, cooling salves, and making small goods look fresher than they are. Its virtue is size, which is not glamorous but often exactly what the job requires.
flavorText: “A large leaf is poor folk’s paper, plate, roof, and fan.”

name: lily pad
shortDescription: A lily pad is a round green raft from quiet water, slick on top and strong enough to host insects, frogs, and the imagination of children. Alchemists use its pond-soaked flesh, while decorators mostly want the calm it suggests.
flavorText: “A lily pad is a small island with no interest in maps.”

name: scarce massive red plant
shortDescription: A scarce massive red plant dominates a stall with thick stems, bold color, and the uncomfortable feeling that it may still be growing. Its bulk makes it costly to move, but dyers and potion-makers pay for the strength held in that red flesh.
flavorText: “Some plants are harvested; this one is subdued.”

name: notable flat stomp
shortDescription: Notable flat stomp is a wide, low-growing plant with thick pads that look designed to survive boots, hooves, and bad luck. Herbalists value its resilient flesh, and trail cooks sometimes use it when all softer greens have given up.
flavorText: “Flat stomp earned its name from everyone who failed to kill it.”

name: orange sunshines
shortDescription: Orange sunshines are bright blooms that seem determined to outdo late afternoon itself. They sell well in festival garlands, cheerful remedies, and market stalls where color matters almost as much as usefulness.
flavorText: “Orange sunshines are proof a flower can shout politely.”

name: fated star
shortDescription: Fated star is a rare star-shaped botanical whose pale points and strange symmetry make it feel more found than grown. Fortune tellers love it, alchemists measure it, and merchants charge extra for any plant that lets buyers blame destiny.
flavorText: “A fated star makes even a weed basket sound prophetic.”

name: palm leaf
shortDescription: A palm leaf is broad, ribbed, and sun-country practical, useful for fans, wraps, mats, roofs, and quick shade in places where shade is almost a currency. Fresh ones smell green and warm, like travel before the dust arrives.
flavorText: “A palm leaf is the desert’s polite answer to too much sky.”

name: notable pink paddies
shortDescription: Notable pink paddies grow in soft clustered pads with a blush color that makes wetland harvest look unexpectedly pretty. They are useful in dyes and gentle tonics, but their real advantage is making customers lean closer before asking the price.
flavorText: “Pink paddies turn marsh work into something court ladies can pretend to understand.”

name: notable saturhino
shortDescription: Notable saturhino is a thick, curious plant with horned growths and a saturated color that looks almost painted on. It is prized by alchemists who enjoy stubborn ingredients and by collectors who enjoy names nobody can explain confidently.
flavorText: “Saturhino sounds invented until the root stains your knife.”

name: notable dephine
shortDescription: Notable dephine is a graceful plant with narrow leaves and a clean scent that lingers after cutting. It has a refined market reputation, appearing in perfumed oils, pale dyes, and remedies sold to buyers who appreciate a pretty label.
flavorText: “Dephine is the herb that learned good posture.”

name: plenny pins
shortDescription: Plenny pins are thin, clustered plant spikes gathered for their sharp scent, stiff texture, and habit of sticking where they are not wanted. They are used in ward bundles, rough brushes, and teas meant to wake people who have ignored gentler methods.
flavorText: “Plenny pins are what plants grow when they are tired of being handled.”

name: small besone
shortDescription: Small besone is a compact plant with neat growth and a mild, green bitterness that lends itself to simple kitchen and healer’s work. It is not impressive at first glance, but dependable ingredients rarely waste energy being dramatic.
flavorText: “Small besone earns repeat buyers, which is better than earning applause.”

name: red padded pawn
shortDescription: Red padded pawn is a low plant with cushioned red growths, soft to the eye and tougher under the knife. It appears in compresses, dyes, and strange little garden displays where people argue whether it is charming or unsettling.
flavorText: “Red padded pawn looks like a cushion for a creature no one has met.”

name: red thorned dramni
shortDescription: Red thorned dramni grows with sharp little defenses and a red tone that warns as much as it decorates. Herbalists harvest it carefully for bitter extracts, while charm-workers prefer branches with the thorns still mean-looking.
flavorText: “Dramni teaches that a beautiful stem may still object.”
